title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about mindfulness and modern life. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Attentive Life
content.subtitle: An article examining the place of mindfulness in contemporary society.
content.text:
Mindfulness — the practice of paying deliberate, non-judgmental attention to the present moment — has travelled a remarkable distance from its origins in Buddhist meditation to its current position as a staple of corporate wellness programmes, hospital waiting rooms, and self-help bestseller lists. [31] Whether this journey represents a genuine democratisation of a valuable psychological practice or a dilution of something profound into a commodity is a question that practitioners, critics, and researchers continue to debate with some intensity. [/31]

The clinical evidence for mindfulness-based interventions is substantial. Mindfulness-Based Cognitive Therapy (MBCT) has been endorsed by health authorities in several countries as an effective treatment for recurrent depression, reducing the risk of relapse in patients who have experienced three or more episodes. [32] Its proponents are careful to note, however, that the therapeutic context matters enormously: the eight-week MBCT programme involves significant instruction, practice, and reflection, and bears little resemblance to a smartphone app promising calm in three minutes. [/32]

The commercialisation of mindfulness has attracted criticism from two directions. From practitioners with roots in Buddhist tradition, the objection is primarily spiritual: what is marketed as mindfulness is often stripped of the ethical framework — the concern for others, the examination of desire and suffering — that gives the practice its depth. [33] From secular critics, the concern is more political: a practice that teaches people to adjust their relationship to stress rather than challenge its sources can, in effect, function as a tool of social compliance, making people more tolerant of conditions that should be changed. [/33]

This critique has been most forcefully applied to corporate mindfulness programmes. When a company introduces meditation classes to help employees manage workplace stress, it may be addressing a genuine need — but it is also, its critics argue, helping workers adapt to a demanding environment rather than questioning why that environment is so demanding. [34] The sociologist Ronald Purser, who coined the term 'McMindfulness', argues that corporate mindfulness systematically avoids any analysis of the structural causes of the suffering it aims to soothe. [/34]

Defenders of mainstream mindfulness respond that these objections, while philosophically interesting, miss the practical point. People suffer from anxiety, depression, and stress in the present tense. Teaching them skills to manage that suffering need not preclude broader social analysis, and waiting for perfect conditions before offering help is a form of cruelty dressed as principle. [35] The individual and the structural, they argue, are not competitors — both require attention, and both can be held at once. [/35]

What seems clear from the research is that mindfulness, practised with genuine commitment and appropriate guidance, produces measurable improvements in psychological wellbeing. [36] What is equally clear is that the word 'mindfulness' now covers a spectrum from evidence-based clinical intervention to branded relaxation products, and that collapsing these very different things into a single category does a disservice to both. [/36]

questions.question: In the opening paragraph, the writer presents mindfulness's journey from Buddhism to mainstream culture as
options: A) a straightforwardly positive development that has helped many people.
B) something that researchers and practitioners agree has been beneficial.
C) a controversial transformation whose value is still being actively debated.
D) a process that has strengthened rather than weakened its original principles.
questions.explanation: The writer says whether this represents genuine democratisation or dilution 'is a question that practitioners, critics, and researchers continue to debate with some intensity' — clearly contested.

questions.question: Supporters of clinical mindfulness programmes warn that
options: A) mindfulness is only effective for patients who have experienced depression.
B) smartphone apps are more effective than in-person mindfulness courses.
C) the clinical form of mindfulness requires substantial commitment and differs greatly from app-based versions.
D) mindfulness should not be recommended until more clinical trials have been completed.
questions.explanation: Proponents note the MBCT programme 'involves significant instruction, practice, and reflection, and bears little resemblance to a smartphone app promising calm in three minutes'.

questions.question: The secular criticism of mindfulness is that it may
options: A) undermine religious traditions that have developed the practice over centuries.
B) encourage people to accept harmful circumstances rather than work to change them.
C) be used by corporations to avoid legal responsibility for employee wellbeing.
D) create unrealistic expectations of inner peace that most people cannot achieve.
questions.explanation: Secular critics worry that mindfulness can 'function as a tool of social compliance, making people more tolerant of conditions that should be changed' — adapting to rather than challenging problems.

questions.question: Ronald Purser's concept of 'McMindfulness' refers to
options: A) a simplified, fast-food version of mindfulness that has become globally popular.
B) corporate mindfulness programmes that ignore the underlying causes of workplace stress.
C) the commercialisation of mindfulness through app development and branded products.
D) mindfulness techniques that have been adapted specifically for business environments.
questions.explanation: Purser argues that corporate mindfulness 'systematically avoids any analysis of the structural causes of the suffering it aims to soothe' — addressing symptoms without examining workplace conditions.

questions.question: Defenders of mainstream mindfulness respond to critics by arguing that
options: A) personal wellbeing and social change can both be pursued without contradiction.
B) clinical research proves that mindfulness is more effective than social action.
C) philosophical objections are irrelevant to people suffering in the present.
D) social change can only happen once individuals have developed inner resilience.
questions.explanation: Defenders say 'The individual and the structural...are not competitors — both require attention, and both can be held at once', arguing personal and social approaches are compatible.

questions.question: The writer's conclusion in the final paragraph is that
options: A) only clinical forms of mindfulness should be available to the public.
B) the benefits of mindfulness have been greatly exaggerated by the media.
C) using 'mindfulness' as a single term for very different things creates confusion.
D) evidence-based mindfulness programmes are always more effective than commercial ones.
questions.explanation: The writer says 'collapsing these very different things into a single category does a disservice to both', meaning treating clinical and commercial mindfulness as one thing creates a misleading picture.
